papers.title: Kinematics in galactic tidal tails - A source for Hypervelocity stars?
papers.author_names: Tilmann Piffl Mary Williams Matthias Steinmetz

abstract.text: [abridged] In this work we study in detail the kinematics of tidal debris stars to investigate the implications of the new scenario that the observed sample of Hypervelocity stars could partly originate from a dwarf-host galaxy collision. We use a suite of N-body simulations following the encounter of a satellite galaxy with its Milky Way-type host galaxy to gather statistics on the properties of stripped-off stars. We study especially the orbital energy distribution of this population.\\\n  We quantify the typical pattern in angular and phase space formed by the debris stars. We further develop a simple stripping model predicting the kinematics of stripped-off stars. We show that the distribution of orbital energies in the tidal debris has a typical form which can be described quite accurately by a simple function. Based on this we develop a method to predict the energy distribution which allows us to evaluate the significance and the implications of high velocity stars in satellite tidal debris.\\\n  Generally tidal collisions of satellite galaxy produce stars which escape into the intragalactic space even if the satellite itself is on a bound orbit. The main parameters determining the maximum energy kick a tidal debris star can get is the initial mass of the satellite and only to a lower extent its orbit. Main contributors to an unbound stellar population created in this way are massive satellites (M_sat > 10^9 M_sun). We thus expect intragalactic stars to have a metallicity higher than the surviving satellite population of the Milky Way. However, the probability that the observed HVS population is significantly contaminated by tidal debris stars appears small in the light of our results.

brief.tldr: [research paper] N-body simulations of satellite galaxy tidal disruption reveal that stripped stellar debris can achieve high orbital energies, with a characteristic energy distribution governed by satellite mass rather than orbit. The kinematics of tidal debris stars show a distinct phase-space pattern, and while massive satellites (>10⁹ M⊙) produce unbound stars capable of reaching hypervelocity regimes, the probability that observed hypervelocity stars are contamination from tidal debris remains low due to energy distribution mismatches and metallicity constraints.

source_fields.title: Kinematics in galactic tidal tails - A source for Hypervelocity stars?

source_fields.sections_text: Summary: This study shows that tidal debris from satellite galaxies forms a confined stellar population with limited ejection velocities, and that hypervelocity stars are unlikely to originate from tidal stripping due to the required massive progenitors and kinematic inconsistencies, while most intergalactic stellar population stems from satellite galaxies with masses above $10^9$ M⊙. Discussion: The experiment shows that only satellite galaxies with masses exceeding $10^{10}$ M⊙ can eject hypervelocity stars (HVSs) with velocities comparable to observed HVSs, and the kinematical properties of the HVS subsample with travel times of ~133 Myr imply a progenitor mass of at least $10^{11}$ M⊙, making a satellite origin for these stars highly unlikely. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that explains the observed clustering in travel time and anisotropic angular distribution of hypervelocity stars not by Hills' original SMBH ejection mechanism, but through a starburst-induced transient increase in ejection rate, thereby solving the discrepancy between theoretical predictions and observational peculiarities in the HVS population. Simulation set-up: The simulation set-up uses Gadget-2 to model a main galaxy with an NFW halo, exponential disk, and Hernquist bulge, and satellite galaxies constrained by observational relations from the Fundamental Plane, fully determined by total mass through a derived mass-concentration relation. The energy distribution: This section describes the energy distribution of tidally stripped stellar particles, characterized by two peaks corresponding to tidal arms and a central gap at the satellite's orbital energy, with an empirical fitting function used to model the high-energy tail where hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are expected. Appendix B Scaling tests: The scaling tests show that the energy distributions and kinematic properties of the satellite system are robust to changes in numerical resolution and softening length, with minimal differences in key quantities. A model for tidal stripping: The paper proposes a simplified model of satellite accretion that treats tidal stripping during pericentric passage as an instantaneous energy transfer process, where stars gain orbital energy relative to the host galaxy's potential due to their velocity relative to the satellite, with the core idea being that the energy gain depends on the star's velocity and the satellite's motion, leading to a net energy increase for stripped particles. Appendix D Initial conditions: This section presents a set of initial conditions for satellite systems, varying parameters such as mass, orbit type, and resolution, to study their dynamical evolution and stability. Appendix C Other host galaxy potentials: The shape of the host galaxy potential has a minor influence on satellite tidal tail dynamics, with only subtle changes in the central energy distribution due to differences in the Roche radius evolution. Observable properties from the simulations: The simulated Most Energetic Particles (MEPs) are concentrated in a small, compact region on the sky with an average angular radius of 16°, reflecting a distinct angular distribution that is consistent with the observed properties of high-velocity stars in the Milky Way. Appendix A Estimating the maximum energy gain: This section details the methodology for estimating the pericenter velocity $V_{\\rm peri}$ and escape velocity $v_{\\rm esc}(R_{\\rm tidal})$ of a satellite galaxy during tidal stripping, accounting for dynamical friction, a two-step tidal radius calculation based on dark matter and baryonic mass loss, and the resulting mass expulsion fraction.

title: Kinematics in galactic tidal tails - A source for Hypervelocity stars?

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents N-body simulations and theoretical analysis of tidal debris kinematics; no observational stellar data or catalog mentioned
